nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, sheltered reef flats, lagoons, and bedrock grooves with moderate to bright light and good water movement; typically found at depths of 2–20 m

decorPreferences: stable rockwork or sandbed for skeletal attachment
position away from direct laminar flow
areas with good water circulation to prevent detritus accumulation

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae housed in the polyp tissue. Has been documented to benefit from supplemental weekly feedings of phytoplankton, microplankton, rotifers, cyclops plankton, or other fine particulate foods suitable for filter-feeding invertebrates. Target feeding via pipette or turkey baster near open polyps is generally recommended.

avoidWith: corals with long sweeper tentacles (e.g., Hammer, Torch, Frogspawn)
aggressive LPS corals in direct contact
filamentous algae and cyanobacteria (can overgrow skeleton)
strong direct laminar flow (damages fragile polyps)

summary: Pipe Organ Coral (Tubipora musica) is an iconic Indo-Pacific octocoral distinguished by its vivid red calcareous skeleton of parallel organ-pipe tubes. CITES Appendix II. Intermediate care; sensitive to algae fouling.
careNotes:
Taxonomy note: Despite producing a rigid red calcareous skeleton, Tubipora musica is classified in Order Malacalcyonacea (octocorals), not Scleractinia (true stony corals). It is the only species in the monotypic genus Tubipora and the family Tubiporidae. The red coloration of the skeleton comes from iron salts in the calcium carbonate — it is not a pigment produced by the polyps themselves. In life, the skeleton is largely obscured by the soft tissue and expanded polyps; the distinctive red tubes are typically only visible when polyps retract.

Red calcareous skeleton biology: The skeleton consists of numerous parallel hollow tubes of calcium carbonate running perpendicular to the substrate, connected at intervals by horizontal platforms (stolons). Each tube houses a single polyp bearing eight pinnate (feather-like) tentacles characteristic of all octocorals. Growth is extremely slow by reef coral standards. The skeleton requires adequate calcium and alkalinity for continued development; instability in these parameters can stall growth or cause skeletal degradation.

Algae-fouling failure mode: The primary husbandry challenge unique to Tubipora musica is the propensity for algae — particularly filamentous algae, cyanobacteria, and turf algae — to colonize the exposed calcareous skeleton between polyp tubes. Once algae establishes, it can spread across the skeleton surface and physically block polyps from extending, effectively suffocating sections of the colony. Preventive management includes: (1) maintaining good water quality with low nitrate and phosphate, (2) ensuring sufficient water movement across the colony to dislodge settling detritus, (3) active herbivore populations (turbo snails, hermit crabs, urchins), and (4) manual removal of any algae present before it spreads. Avoid placing this coral near algae-prone areas of the tank.

Water flow nuance: Sources disagree on ideal flow intensity. LiveAquaria and most hobbyist sources recommend medium intermittent flow, avoiding direct strong currents that damage the fragile polyps. Tidal Gardens recommends higher flow specifically to dislodge detritus from the skeletal tubes. In practice, gentle chaotic or randomized flow (wavemaker-style, not laminar) is generally regarded as the best approach — enough to remove detritus from skeletal crevices without stressing the polyps.

Lighting: Sources vary considerably on PAR — Tidal Gardens recommends 75–100 PAR, Reeflings 50–150 PAR, and ReefChasers 300–350 PAR (an outlier that may reflect conditions this coral can tolerate rather than optimal range). The majority of practical experience and scientific literature places this species as a moderate-light coral. Avoid both extremes: too little light causes the zooxanthellae to underperform; too much risks bleaching of the soft tissue. Acclimate gradually when moving colonies to new lighting conditions.

CITES Appendix II: Tubipora musica is regulated under CITES Appendix II, meaning international trade requires documentation demonstrating the specimen was legally obtained. Aquacultured specimens (frags) are available and strongly preferred, as wild collection has contributed significantly to population declines. The species is considered near-threatened due to harvest pressure for aquariums, jewelry, and traditional medicine.

Skeletal supplements: Sources consistently recommend regular supplementation of calcium, magnesium, strontium, iodine, and other trace elements to support skeletal integrity. Target calcium ~380–450 ppm and magnesium ~1250–1400 ppm, consistent with a standard mixed-reef parameter regime.

Growth habit: Unlike Green Star Polyps or Xenia, Tubipora musica has a notably slow and contained growth habit — it will not spread aggressively across rockwork or crowd neighboring corals. This makes it a more manageable occupant in a mixed reef.
breedingNotes: Captive propagation by fragging is possible but reportedly challenging compared to most soft corals, given the rigid calcareous skeleton. Frags are typically secured with reef-safe adhesive or epoxy. Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning; deliberate sexual breeding in captivity is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Pipe Organ Coral (Tubipora musica)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.025), dKH (8–12), lighting (moderate to high), flow (medium), care level (moderate), and feeding notes. Tank size not specified.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Pipe Organ Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked: PAR 75–100 (lower than ReefChasers 300–350 outlier); calcium ~425 ppm; magnesium ~1350 ppm; alkalinity 8–9 dKH typical. Recommended high flow to remove detritus from skeletal tubes. Notes slow growth rate and crustacean colonization of skeleton. Native range: Pacific, Australia, Indonesia.

additional.name: Reeflings – Organ Pipe Coral (Tubipora musica) Complete Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, PAR 50–150 (consistent with moderate interpretation), flow moderate. Care level moderate to easy. Indo-Pacific native range, depth 25 m max. Confirmed zooxanthellae photosynthetic primary nutrition with microplankton supplementation.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory – Organ Pipe Coral (Tubipora musica) Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 77–82°F (25–27.8°C, higher than LiveAquaria); colony can reach 12 in high x 24 in across (30 x 60 cm) in aquarium; algae fouling of skeleton documented as primary failure mode; notes chaotic flow preferred but difficult in home aquariums. Indian Ocean native range cited.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Tubipora musica
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Tubiporidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea), native range (Indian Ocean, central-western Pacific, south of Japan, west to East Africa, Red Sea), depth 2–20 m, wild colony up to 3 m across, and CITES near-threatened status. Red skeleton iron-salt pigmentation confirmed.
